name: Pandion
tagline: Machine learning-powered parcel delivery network for e-commerce residential shipping
description: Pandion was a Bellevue, Washington-based parcel logistics startup founded in 2020 by former Amazon Air executive Scott Ruffin. The company used machine learning to dynamically route packages through a network of five sortation centers, picking from a marketplace of 500,000+ final-mile drivers and partners like USPS. Pandion raised roughly $125 million across multiple rounds — including a $41.5M Series B in March 2024 — and delivered over 100,000 packages a day. It shut down abruptly on January 10, 2025, less than a year after that Series B, after talks with potential acquirers failed and the company ran out of cash.
story:
Pandion was supposed to be the AI-native answer to UPS and FedEx. Founder Scott Ruffin had spent years building Amazon Air — the e-commerce giant's in-house air cargo network — before leaving in 2020 to start a company that would use machine learning to crack the residential 'middle mile.' The premise was simple but technically ambitious: instead of running trucks and drivers, Pandion would operate sortation centers and let an ML system decide, for each package, which final-mile provider would deliver it fastest and cheapest. By 2024 the network spanned Los Angeles, Dallas, Atlanta, Chicago, and Philadelphia, and the company claimed to reach more than 80% of U.S. homes through 500,000+ drivers from USPS, regional carriers, and gig platforms.

Investors bought into the vision. Pandion raised roughly $125 million in equity over five years, including $30 million in 2021 and a $41.5 million Series B in March 2024 led by SoftBank Vision Fund 2, with Bloomberg reporting sales were on track to hit $220 million in 2024. The company was named one of Retail Brew's startups to watch. But the e-commerce parcel market shifted hard against new entrants: rates compressed, Amazon expanded its own logistics, and the funding environment for capital-intensive logistics startups dried up. By late 2024, Pandion had cash to last only through the end of the year.

On January 10, 2025, Ruffin gathered his 63 employees on a Friday afternoon and told them the company was shutting down immediately. Talks with potential acquirers had collapsed. There was no money for severance — the company owed more than it had in the bank — and staff were paid only through January 15. All five sortation centers and the Bellevue headquarters closed within days. Investors who had poured $125 million into the bet that AI-driven routing could disrupt parcel delivery were left with nothing.

sources.title: Heavily funded Pandion delivery startup closes abruptly in latest logistics industry fallout
Pandion closes after burning through $41m funding
Pandion Secures $41.5M in Series B Funding
